title: June 2026 Newsletter | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

A new report by Centrum Cyfrowe and Jagiellonian University's Future Law Lab finds that Europe's fragmented copyright system hinders researchers. At the heart of the report is the newly developed Index of Research Exceptions (I-REx), a comparative tool that shows that, on average, all EU Member States have implemented research exceptions more restrictively than required under EU law, more than twice as restrictively as the EU baseline.

With the European Research Area (ERA) Act taking shape and EU copyright reform on the horizon, the report calls for copyright law to facilitate, not obstruct, scientific research and for the right to research to become a core component of the ERA. The report benefited from support from KR21 and COMMUNIA.

Report page | Blog post

Calling all higher education institutions across Europe: KR21 project partner SPARC Europe has launched the 2026 edition of its Open Education survey to take stock of progress. Without systematic data, Open Education remains invisible in policy discussions. 10 to 15 minutes of your time helps build the evidence base.

Take the survey by 20 July 2026 (18:00 CEST)

title: May 2026 Newsletter | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

As the EU's review of copyright rules gathers pace, the voices of the research, education and cultural heritage communities must speak up. A detailed survey on the Copyright in the Digital Single Market (CDSM) Directive closes on 14 June (extended deadline). This survey is part of the twin consultations on the CDSM review and the future of the EU's copyright framework. The other consultation, an open Call for Evidence, closes on 25 June.

Together, our voices can shape copyright rules in Europe that truly serve research, education, cultural heritage and the libraries that support them.

Calling all French library professionals: KR21 national coordinators for France, Camille Françoise and Ophélie Wang, want to hear from you! They have launched a survey on public library access to e-Book licences, pricing, and acquisition conditions. The goal: build an evidence base for a fairer e-Book market in which libraries can fully fulfil their mission. 15–20 minutes of your time could make a real difference.

Share your input by 1 July
snippet: View this email in your browser

title: Shape EU Copyright Reform, Part 2: A Guide to the Open Call for Evidence (+ Survey Extended to 14 June) | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

Last week, we pointed you to the detailed survey on the Copyright in the Digital Single Market (CDSM) Directive (extended deadline: 14 June) and the KR21 answering guide. This week, we turn our attention to the second of the EU’s twin consultations: the European Commission’s (EC) open Call for Evidence on the future of copyright reform (closing on 25 June).

This consultation gives you far more freedom to highlight issues that are not yet adequately addressed by EU law. You can do this in up to 4,000 words via the EC’s consultation platform, or by attaching longer documents to your response.

Your input shapes what comes next. KR21 prepared an answer guide for the call, outlining 11 different asks you could make. Highlight as many as you wish, and add your own.

If you have any questions, do not hesitate to contact us at info@knowledgerights21.org.

  • Focus: The real-world impact of the CDSM Directive. What works, what doesn't.

    • Focus: The real-world impact of the CDSM Directive. What works, what doesn't.

  • Format: Detailed survey with space to share your experience and views

    • Format: Detailed survey with space to share your experience and views

title: Shape EU Copyright Reform: Guide to the Copyright in the Digital Single Market Directive Review Survey | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

As the EU's review of copyright rules gathers pace, the voices of the research, education and cultural heritage communities need to be heard. A detailed survey on the Copyright in the Digital Single Market (CDSM) Directive closes on 5 June.

Your input will make a real difference. While completing the survey, you can use KR21’s answer guide, which highlights key points and topics to consider. Your response should reflect your own experience and views.

This survey is part of the twin consultations currently open on the CDSM review and the future of the EU's copyright framework, discussed in more depth in this recent KR21 blog post. The other consultation, an open Call for Evidence, closes on 25 June. Look out for materials from us to help you respond.

  • Focus: The real-world impact of the CDSM Directive. What works, what doesn't.

    • Focus: The real-world impact of the CDSM Directive. What works, what doesn't.

title: April 2026 Newsletter | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

Publishers refuse to licence digital titles to libraries. Contracts override rights granted by law. Digital locks block preservation that copyright explicitly permits. These are not edge cases: they are the everyday reality for libraries across Europe.

On World Book and Copyright Day (23 April), KR21 launched a living case study repository to complement its Let Libraries be Libraries campaign and its action plan to empower libraries. These initial examples address the nine barriers identified in the plan. Each case study is structured around the facts, the impact, and the need for change. Every action point now also includes curated background resources to support your advocacy efforts.

If you work in or with a library and have encountered any of these barriers, submit your case study through the form on the Library Action Plan page or by email. A submission does not need to be long or formally written. It just needs to be real.

Explore the cases & share your story | Read the blog | Download the Action Plan
snippet: View this email in your browser

title: March 2026 Newsletter | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

National experience across seven EU Member States tells a clear story: Secondary Publication Rights (SPRs) work best not as exceptions to copyright, but as instruments of research governance and author empowerment. As the European Commission shapes the European Research Area (ERA) Act, this KR21 Insights report makes the case for placing SPR in research legislation, offering greater certainty, a framework centred on researchers, and a direct contribution to the 5th Freedom, the free movement of knowledge.

Learn more

Do you work in a university, higher education, or research library in Europe (or beyond)? Share your experience on the role of libraries in research-industry collaboration. The results will feed directly into a KR21 report informing our advocacy for stronger library rights and open science across the EU. Ten questions, anonymous, and just a few minutes of your time.

Answer now

title: February 2026 Newsletter | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

The mission of libraries is under threat. Not just from budget cuts or censorship, but from a “silent” crisis: a legal and market framework that is systematically stripping libraries of their right to function. Libraries are being written out of the digital age. It’s time to write them back in.

On 19 February 2026, Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) launched a definitive roadmap to fight back, outlining 9 actions to empower libraries. KR21 calls on the European Union and national governments to adopt a comprehensive strategy across two critical fronts:

  • End market mismatch

    • End market mismatch

  • Modernise copyright for today

    • Modernise copyright for today

    This Action Plan isn’t just a policy paper: it’s a tool for librarians, institutions, and library associations. Governments rely on libraries to deliver on policy commitments, but they need to hear clearly what is standing in our way.

    Go discover the KR21 Action Plan and materials, and help spread the word!

    This 19 February webinar marked the launch of KR21’s Action Plan to restore libraries' rights in the digital age.

    A must-watch for:
snippet: View this email in your browser

title: WEBINAR: 19 February (11:00 CET) - Enabling Libraries, Guaranteeing Rights | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

Libraries lack a legal framework that truly supports their mission and empowers their work. To address this, KR21 invites you to a webinar on enabling libraries and guaranteeing their rights. The event marks the official launch of KR21's 9-point Action Plan, "Safeguard Access, Empower Europe — An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries," a checklist of the changes needed in the digital age to ensure libraries benefit from the same possibilities online as offline. Read more

For this launch, we’re joined by experts from the community:

  • Beatrice Murch | Internet Archive Europe

    • Beatrice Murch | Internet Archive Europe

  • Mina Charalambous | Cyprus Library Association

    • Mina Charalambous | Cyprus Library Association

  • Anna Tuomikoski | National Library of Finland, National eLibrary

    • Anna Tuomikoski | National Library of Finland, National eLibrary

  • Cathal McCauley | Maynooth University

    • Cathal McCauley | Maynooth University

  • Deborah De Angelis | KR21 National Coordinator for Italy

    • Deborah De Angelis | KR21 National Coordinator for Italy

    Moderation by Stephen Wyber | KR21 Programme Lead/IFLA

title: January 2026 Newsletter | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

Europe’s 100,000+ libraries serve millions of students, researchers, and citizens, yet unfair contract terms undermine their mission. However, libraries often cannot acquire content on reasonable terms because of a stark imbalance of power with publishers. Libraries exist to serve consumers, yet they fall outside the scope of consumer protection laws available to their users. Let’s fix this!

Learn more

Join us at the Academy of Sciences of Lisbon in Portugal for a conference co-organised by KR21 on secondary publication rights (SPR), featuring keynote speeches by Manuel Aleixo from the Cabinet of European Commissioner Ekaterina Zaharieva and Shaofeng Hu from UNESCO.

Details | Register now

Join the Community of Practice, driven by KR21 project partner SPARC Europe, in welcoming speakers from Finland and Germany to share their experiences and perspectives.

Details | Save your spot

title: December 2025 Newsletter | Knowledge Rights 21
content:
View this email in your browser

Stay tuned for more information on the upcoming 8th meeting of this online collaborative space, driven by KR21 project partner SPARC Europe, in February 2026! Join the movement and retain your rights: sign up for community updates and more.

Tech Policy Press features an analysis of the European Commission’s Digital Omnibus by KR21’s Programme Lead Stephen Wyber. While this omnibus has already sparked intense debate, Stephen points out what has been overlooked: the critical impact on research institutions and libraries, despite the EC’s commitment to boosting research in Europe. With negotiations on the digital omnibus starting, there’s still a chance to do better! The research community must voice its concerns now to ensure that enabling research becomes a first-order priority in EU digital legislation.

Discover the 10 key takeaways
